senses.definition: of or relating to an area roughly one-quarter of a tertian division; used rarely in mathematical or technical contexts to describe a fractional relation to a three-part division.
examples: The sesquitertianal partition divided the shape into a quarter of a tertian segment.
In the diagram, the sesquitertianal region is shown in shading distinct from the whole.

senses.definition: plural of sesquitertia, a historical or technical term referring to a fractional unit of measure equal to one quarter of a tertia.
examples: The table lists sesquitertias alongside other ancient monetary units.
Architects referenced sesquitertias to describe fractional divisions.

senses.definition: short form for session or a meeting; also a short form in computing for a session identifier.
examples: The training sess lasted two hours.
Your login sess is stored in the browser.

senses.definition: to sit; in some dialects or informal writing, to place oneself in a seated position.
examples: They sess by the fire while waiting.
She sessed quietly at the edge of the room.

senses.definition: proper noun or surname; rare given name in some cultures.
examples: Sessa published a study on coastal ecosystems.
The village of Sessa is known for its ceramics.

senses.definition: having been seated; possessed of a seat or being seated.
examples: The sassed audience remained calm after the announcement.
A sassed chair was placed near the stage.

senses.definition: third-person singular present of 'to sess' (see above) or a modern misspelling; in standard usage, not common.
examples: He sesses by the window during the break.

senses.definition: not moving; attached directly by the base; fixed in one place.
examples: The sessile colonies of coral are attached to rocks.
A sessile leaf lies flat against the stem.

senses.definition: the quality or state of being sessile; lack of movement or mobility.
examples: Sessilities in the plant stem were remarkable under the microscope.

senses.definition: the state of being sessile; tendency to remain fixed in one place; in biology, the fixed attachment of an organism or structure.
examples: Sessility is common in barnacles that attach to rocks.
The organism exhibits sessility, lacking mobility.

senses.definition: a taxonomic or descriptive term referencing a sessile (attached) abdomen region in some invertebrates; technical usage
examples: The specimen shows sessiliventres in the larval stage.

senses.definition: a period of time when people meet for a particular purpose; a formal meeting or series of meetings.
examples: We have a long study session after dinner.
The court will begin a new session tomorrow.

senses.definition: relating to or used during a session, especially in an academic term; existing in a class session.
examples: Students submit their assignments in a sessional deadline.
A sessional lecturer taught the course this term.

senses.definition: in a manner related to a session or term; with respect to a school or parliamentary term.
examples: The courses are offered sessionally in the autumn term.

senses.definition: an entity or person that attends a session; a participant in a meeting or legislative session.
examples: The sessionary raised a point during the debate.

senses.definition: plural of session; multiple periods of meeting or study.
examples: They had several study sessions before the exam.
The court held three sessions this month.

senses.definition: a superficial or informal term for a stagnant pool of water that collects in a sessal area; not common in standard usage.
examples: The puddle became a sesspool after the rain.

senses.definition: plural of sesspool; uncommon.
examples: The garden features several sesspools around the patio.

senses.definition: an ancient Roman bronze or copper coin; a unit of currency used in antiquity.
examples: A coin from the Roman sesterce denomination was found at the site.

senses.definition: plural of sesterce; ancient Roman coins used as money.
examples: The collection included several sesterces from different eras.

senses.definition: plural of sestertium, a large Roman coin or unit of account; historically used for large sums.
examples: The estimate was in sestertia, reflecting a vast ancient amount.

senses.definition: plural of sestertius; the large Roman coin or unit of account.
examples: In ancient texts, prices are often given in sestertii.

senses.definition: a large Roman unit of money or accounting, sometimes defined as a multiple of lesser coins.
examples: Records mention the cost in sestertia to emphasize wealth.

senses.definition: the Roman bronze coin of the value 1/4 as or sometimes more; the singular form of sestertii.
examples: The artifact bore a worn sestertius.

senses.definition: a group of six lines of verse in a poem; a six-line stanza.
examples: The sonnet ends with a sestet that mirrors the octave.
She wrote a cheerful sestet to finish the poem.

senses.definition: plural of sestet; multiple six-line verses in poetry.
examples: The poem comprises three sestets.
